channel.title: Emily Darlington MP
channel.description: Championing a Fairer, Greener, and Brighter Future for Milton Keynes Central

item.title: Pre-Loved Fashion, Life-Changing Care: Willen Hospice Comes to Westminster
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: I was proud to welcome Willen Hospice to Parliament for a special charity shop pop-up in the Jubilee Room – bringing a vital piece of Milton Keynes and the spirit of ‘No Buying New’ to Westminster, while shining a spotlight on a cause that matters deeply to our community. For many people in Milton Keynes, […]
item.content:encoded:
I was proud to welcome Willen Hospice to Parliament for a special charity shop pop-up in the Jubilee Room – bringing a vital piece of Milton Keynes and the spirit of ‘No Buying New’ to Westminster, while shining a spotlight on a cause that matters deeply to our community.

For many people in Milton Keynes, Willen Hospice is there at the moments that matter most – providing care, comfort and support to both patients and their families. But like hospices across the country, it faces growing financial pressures, and that’s why its innovative approach to fundraising is so important.

With 15 shops across our region, Willen Hospice generates more than a quarter of its income through retail, offering everything from high street fashion to specialist experiences like Off the Record and its unique clearance outlet in Stacey Bushes. These shops give people the opportunity to shop sustainably, reducing waste and supporting a more conscious way of consuming.

Hosting the pop-up in Parliament was about more than just great finds (although there were plenty!). It was about telling the story of how community generosity, through donations and purchases alike, directly supports hospice care for local people. Every item sold helps ensure that patients and their loved ones continue to receive the care they deserve.

I’ve personally been a long-time supporter of shopping second-hand, and I was delighted to see colleagues in Parliament taking inspiration and exploring charity shops in their own constituencies too. It’s a simple shift, but one that can make a real difference.

At a time when nearly 60% of hospices are facing cuts to frontline services, we must do more to recognise and support the role they play in healthcare, and our communities. Initiatives like this show that sustainable shopping and local giving can go hand in hand, helping to secure the future of hospice care for generations to come.

item.title: Standing Together to Champion Women and Girls in Milton Keynes
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: International Women’s Day is always an important moment in the calendar, but this year’s gathering in Milton Keynes felt especially powerful. I was honoured to host a community event bringing together residents, professionals, and members of the Dawoodi Bohra community to celebrate the achievements of women and to reflect on the work still needed to […]
item.content:encoded:
International Women’s Day is always an important moment in the calendar, but this year’s gathering in Milton Keynes felt especially powerful. I was honoured to host a community event bringing together residents, professionals, and members of the Dawoodi Bohra community to celebrate the achievements of women and to reflect on the work still needed to ensure equality, respect, and safety for all.

One of the most powerful aspects of the event was hearing directly from women working across different sectors. Police officers, detectives, and business leaders joined us to share their journeys – speaking candidly about the challenges they have faced and the resilience required to overcome them.

Participants were able to ask questions about navigating their careers, responding to difficult situations, and building inclusive workplaces. These conversations were open and honest, reminding us that while progress has been made, many women still encounter barriers in professional and public life.

A central theme of the discussion was the importance of the White Ribbon campaign, a global movement that encourages people – particularly men – to take a stand against violence towards women. This campaign is deeply personal to me. In my previous work in Milton Keynes, I helped lead the effort that saw our city become the first White Ribbon City in the UK, and I continue to champion this work nationally. The message is simple but powerful: we must never use, excuse, or remain silent about violence against women.

Alongside discussion, attendees took part in a creative activity – designing posters about women’s rights and raising awareness of issues affecting women and girls. It was inspiring to see participants transform ideas into messages that could help spark wider conversations across our community.

I was particularly grateful for the participation of the Dawoodi Bohra community in Milton Keynes. Their presence reflected a shared commitment to dignity, service, and social responsibility – values that resonate strongly across faiths and cultures.

Events like this remind us that progress comes not only through policy, but through dialogue, education, and communities working together. I’m grateful to everyone who joined us and helped make it such a meaningful and inspiring occasion.

item.title: Our Children Deserve Better Online: What My Survey Revealed
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Thank you to everyone who took part in my recent survey on online safety. The strength of feeling was clear, young people are concerned about the harmful content that they encounter online: from self-harm and suicide material, to violent and exploitative content – but just as clear was the message that a blanket ban on […]
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to everyone who took part in my recent survey on online safety. The strength of feeling was clear, young people are concerned about the harmful content that they encounter online: from self-harm and suicide material, to violent and exploitative content – but just as clear was the message that a blanket ban on social media is not the solution.

While many support stronger age protections, most respondents recognised that banning young people from platforms altogether does not address the root cause of the problem. Children are growing up in a digital world. Simply removing them from it does not make that world safer, it allows social media companies to avoid confronting the way their platforms are designed.

We must not allow tech companies to forget their responsibility. If harmful content is being pushed toward children, that is a failure of systems and safeguards – not simply parenting.

It was an honour to speak with Ian Russell about the meaningful changes needed to protect young people online. His work has been instrumental in driving this debate forward, and his message is clear: no parent should have to live in fear of the content their child may see.

I also strongly recommend reading the “Roadmap for a better online future” from the Molly Rose Foundation, which sets out practical steps to strengthen regulation, improve transparency, and ensure child safety is built into platform design from the start.

My survey results demonstrate that the public want action, but they want the right action. Stronger enforcement, greater accountability, and safer systems, not simple solutions that fail to tackle the real problem.

item.title: Are You Eligible? Final Days for Warm Homes Discount Support
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Nobody in our city should be worrying about how they are heating their home – especially at a time when energy costs are still putting pressure on household budgets. That’s why I want to remind everyone in Milton Keynes that the Warm Homes Discount is still available, but the deadline to check your eligibility or […]
item.content:encoded:
Nobody in our city should be worrying about how they are heating their home – especially at a time when energy costs are still putting pressure on household budgets. That’s why I want to remind everyone in Milton Keynes that the Warm Homes Discount is still available, but the deadline to check your eligibility or apply is this Friday.

This year the scheme has been expanded under the Labour Government, meaning even more families can access this vital support. More than 6 million homes across the country are now entitled to receive a one-off £150 discount to help with the cost of heating their homes, giving essential support with the cost of living.

For most households on means-tested benefits the discount will be applied automatically to your energy bill this winter – making the process easier for everyone. But for a small number of people who have not received a letter telling them they will receive the discount, there’s still time to act. If you think you are eligible but haven’t had the discount applied or received a letter, please call 08000309322 by Friday to make sure you receive the support you are entitled to.

If you’re unsure whether you qualify, or if you didn’t receive a letter, make that call – it could make a real difference for your household this winter.

Read the full article here: Milton Keynes MPs encourage households to apply for Warm Homes Discount as deadline looms

item.title: A Cleaner, Greener NHS for Milton Keynes
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: I’m delighted to share some truly positive news for our community and our NHS. Recently, it was confirmed that £162,000 of Government funding will go towards clean energy upgrades at Milton Keynes University Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. This funding forms part of a broader initiative supporting nearly 200 NHS sites across the country to lower […]
item.content:encoded:
I’m delighted to share some truly positive news for our community and our NHS.

Recently, it was confirmed that £162,000 of Government funding will go towards clean energy upgrades at Milton Keynes University Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. This funding forms part of a broader initiative supporting nearly 200 NHS sites across the country to lower energy costs and cut carbon emissions – through clean power projects delivered alongside Great British Energy.

Too often, precious NHS resources are absorbed by soaring energy bills instead of being invested where they matter most: frontline services for patients. By installing clean power systems and energy-saving infrastructure, we are helping the hospital keep more of its funding for patient care, not wasteful overheads.

In Milton Keynes, this investment means:

  • Lower energy bills for our hospital – saving money that can be reinvested in services, staff, and equipment.
  • A more sustainable hospital estate, contributing to the NHS’s broader efforts to reduce emissions and become more resilient.
  • Support for our future hospital plans – this project dovetails with the £500 million new hospital due to be built between 2027 and 2030, setting a standard for 21st-century healthcare in our city.

We are seeing a real commitment from this Government – not just to build new healthcare facilities, but to make sure our existing ones work smarter and more efficiently too.

As we implement these improvements locally and push forward with plans for a new hospital for Milton Keynes and North Buckinghamshire, I will continue to champion investments that support our NHS staff and deliver better care for patients.

I want to thank the teams at MKUH and Great British Energy for their commitment to this work – and I look forward to seeing real benefits for our community as this funding is put to work.

Read the full article here: £162,000 clean energy funding for Milton Keynes Hospital – MKFM 106.3FM – Radio Made in Milton Keynes

item.title: Getting Waiting Lists Down in Milton Keynes
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Last week, I had been back at Milton Keynes University Hospital to thank staff for the work they are doing to bring waiting lists down for local patients. It was important to say thank you in person – because the progress we are seeing is down to the dedication and professionalism of NHS staff working […]
item.content:encoded:
Last week, I had been back at Milton Keynes University Hospital to thank staff for the work they are doing to bring waiting lists down for local patients. It was important to say thank you in person – because the progress we are seeing is down to the dedication and professionalism of NHS staff working under sustained pressure.

Despite record demand last year, waiting lists at our local hospital have fallen since the general election. That matters to patients in Milton Keynes Central who are waiting for a diagnosis, treatment, or reassurance that everything is okay.

Across England, NHS waiting lists fell by more than 86,000 in November alone. That progress has been delivered during the busiest year the NHS has ever faced. In 2025, there were 27.8 million A&E attendances, more than 367,000 higher than the previous year, with 2.33 million attendances in December alone. These figures underline just how significant this achievement is.

This month also marks a year since Labour published its Elective Reform Plan, setting out how we would tackle long waits and modernise the NHS. Since then, record investment has been matched with practical reform: more evening and weekend clinics, expanded community diagnostic centres and surgical hubs, and a move towards “straight to test” pathways so patients are not sent back and forth for unnecessary appointments. Expert teams have also been sent into trusts facing the highest pressures, helping to clear backlogs where they are most severe.

Here in Milton Keynes, that approach is making a real difference. Hundreds more patients have received faster diagnoses and quicker treatment, or the all-clear, over the last 12 months. That has happened even as services continue to operate under consistently high pressure.

Winter always brings extra pressure for the NHS, and flu is continuing to have an impact on hospitals and ambulance services. If you are eligible for the flu vaccine in Milton Keynes, please do consider getting it – it is not too late, and it makes a real difference in protecting both individuals and the wider community.

There is still a long road ahead. Years of underinvestment cannot be undone overnight. But what we are seeing now is progress built on a clear plan, proper funding, and trust in NHS staff to deliver. Labour is fixing our health service, not with slogans, but with sustained action – so it is fit for the future and there for everyone who needs it.

item.title: Holding Tech Giants to Account on Deepfakes and Online Abuse
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Last week’s Prime Minister’s Questions was my first official time standing up in the Chamber to put a question directly to the Prime Minister. I raised concerns about the use of Elon Musk’s AI tool, Grok, to create non-consensual sexualised images – including images of women being digitally stripped without their consent. I was glad, […]
item.content:encoded:
Last week’s Prime Minister’s Questions was my first official time standing up in the Chamber to put a question directly to the Prime Minister. I raised concerns about the use of Elon Musk’s AI tool, Grok, to create non-consensual sexualised images – including images of women being digitally stripped without their consent.

I was glad, and reassured, by the Prime Minister’s response. He was absolutely right to say that what has been happening on X is disgusting and shameful, and to make clear that this government will not back down when UK law is being broken.

Stripping women naked without consent – whether in real life or online – is abuse. That should not be controversial, and it should not be optional for tech companies to address. The fact that X initially chose to restrict this behaviour only to paying users was deeply concerning. You cannot monetise abuse and expect it to be acceptable.

Within minutes of my question at PMQs, people were testing Grok by asking it to generate sexualised images of me. Fortunately, it does not appear to have responded, which suggests that changes may already have been made. But that only underlines the point: these harms are real, immediate, and targeted – and women in public life experience them constantly.

What matters most to me is not winning an argument with a tech billionaire, but demonstrating that UK law can and will be enforced. When pressure is applied, these platforms do change their behaviour. That is important, because for too long there has been an assumption that tech companies are untouchable or beyond the reach of democratic accountability. They are not.

This issue is also much bigger than one platform or one tool. The next challenge we face is protecting our elections, tackling deepfakes, and dealing with algorithms that shape what people see and believe – often without transparency or oversight. If we fail to act, trust in our democracy and our online spaces will continue to be eroded.

This is about setting clear boundaries: abuse is abuse, consent matters, and the law applies online just as it does offline. I welcome the Prime Minister’s commitment to action, and I will continue to push for strong enforcement and proper safeguards – because people deserve to feel safe, both online and off.

Read the full article here: Starmer ‘won’t back down’ in fight with Musk over Grok AI deepfakes

item.title: Big Tech sexism must end
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Right now, many women and girls across the country – especially young women – are finding it harder than ever to access honest, medically accurate information about their own bodies. Social media platforms increasingly bury or block content that talks about “periods,” “vagina,” “menopause,” “endometriosis” or other legitimate women’s health topics. This is not a […]
item.content:encoded:
Right now, many women and girls across the country – especially young women – are finding it harder than ever to access honest, medically accurate information about their own bodies. Social media platforms increasingly bury or block content that talks about “periods,” “vagina,” “menopause,” “endometriosis” or other legitimate women’s health topics. This is not a technical glitch, or lack of interest in such topics – it is a form of digital discrimination.

Recently, I brought together activists and parliamentarians to understand the scale of this new iteration of an age-old problem and to seek solutions. The Big Tech companies – TikTok, Meta (which owns Facebook and Instagram), Google, and X – are all aware of this issue. We must make them understand the damage it is doing and urge them to fix it.

A powerful contribution to our understanding of this problem comes from Essity – their 2025 “Ban the Ban” survey found that a large share of users aged 18–34 use social media for health education, yet many – especially 18–24 year olds -find it hard to locate women’s-health information.

The consequences are real. As shown by campaigners including CensHERship, 95 per cent of women’s-health content creators, charities or educators say they have experienced censorship over the past year – from reach-restriction or removal of posts to ad-/account suspensions.

This isn’t a small barrier – it’s a serious threat to public health, equity and access to vital information. When accurate medical content is suppressed, space is left open for misinformation, unsafe advice and profiteers. It also undermines women’s health charities and fem-tech businesses relying on social media reach to survive.

This must change. It is unacceptable that platforms treat medically accurate discussion of the female body as if it were obscene or taboo. I am committed to working with colleagues, campaigners, and regulators to force Big Tech to explain and reform their moderation practices. Platforms have the tools and resources – what they lack is the will.

We must demand transparency, fair moderation standards, and clear appeal mechanisms. Women and girls deserve to access trustworthy information about their own bodies. And we must not allow social media to become a gatekeeper limiting their right to learn.

Because at its core, this isn’t just about social media policy – it’s about respect, equity and the right to health.

Read my full article piece here: ‘Big Tech sexism must end’ – LabourList

item.title: Defence Matters – But We Can’t Sacrifice Britain’s Soft Power
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: Right now, the world feels more unstable than at any time in recent memory. Conflicts are spreading, humanitarian crises are deepening, and climate-driven displacement is intensifying. In such times, Britain’s strength isn’t measured just by military might – our global influence, compassion and ability to lead through values matter just as much. I believe we […]
item.content:encoded:
Right now, the world feels more unstable than at any time in recent memory. Conflicts are spreading, humanitarian crises are deepening, and climate-driven displacement is intensifying. In such times, Britain’s strength isn’t measured just by military might – our global influence, compassion and ability to lead through values matter just as much.

I believe we must invest in defence. But not if it means abandoning our commitment to international development. Redirecting aid resources to boost the defence budget risks damaging the values and global standing that make Britain a force for good on the world stage.

Since 2025, the UK aid budget has been set to fall – shrinking from 0.5 per cent of national income to 0.3 per cent by 2027. This may free up money for defence in the short-term – but the consequences go far beyond numbers on a balance sheet. Vital humanitarian, health and development programmes overseas face collapse.

We all recognise the need for a strong defence – especially in a volatile world. But true security isn’t built on weapons alone. It is grounded in prevention: alleviating poverty, strengthening public health, building resilient communities, and tackling climate-related challenges at their roots. When we abandon that mission, we do more than forgo goodwill – we undermine long-term global stability and, ultimately, our own safety.

That is why I’m calling on my party and my government to chart a clear, realistic path back to the internationally agreed target of 0.7 per cent of GNI for aid. Not overnight – but step by step, with transparency, planning and respect for the vulnerable.

At a time of global peril, Britain should lead with principle, not abandon it.

Read the full article here: Yes, we must invest in defence – but not at the expense of our soft power | The Independent

item.title: Why I’m Urging Ofcom to Hold X Accountable for Antisemitic Hate
item.dc:creator: emilydarlingtonmp
item.description: I recently joined more than 30 of my parliamentary colleagues from across the political spectrum in writing to Ofcom to demand urgent action over a surge in antisemitic posts and calls for violence against Jewish people on X (formerly Twitter). The research, carried out by the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH), exposed vile replies […]
item.content:encoded:
I recently joined more than 30 of my parliamentary colleagues from across the political spectrum in writing to Ofcom to demand urgent action over a surge in antisemitic posts and calls for violence against Jewish people on X (formerly Twitter).

The research, carried out by the Center for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH), exposed vile replies to posts by prominent British Jews after the terrorist attack outside a synagogue in Manchester: content celebrating the killings, glorifying genocide, even urging more violence. Such messages – mainstream hatred, incitement, calls for violence – cross into what the Online Safety Act 2023 categorises as “priority illegal content.”

This is not about silencing free speech. It is about protecting lives, dignity and public safety. As an MP, I believe that hate must have no safe harbour online. Under the law, platforms have a legal duty not only to remove harmful, illegal content, but to design their services in a way that prevents such content from appearing at all.

This moment demands clarity and action. Ofcom must act swiftly and decisively and now to hold X to account. Hate must have no refuge online.

Read the full article here: Politics Home Article | Cross-Party MPs Demand Ofcom Investigation Into Antisemitism On X
